OSCE Station 41I · Head & Neck Anatomy

The Paranasal Sinuses

Structural boundaries · Drainage routes · Innervation · CT & cadaveric correlates

Sinuses4 pairs
BoneFrontal, Maxillary, Ethmoid, Sphenoid
Nerve SupplyCN V₁ & V₂ (trigeminal)
Key DrainageLateral nasal wall → nasal cavity
MucosaPseudostratified ciliated columnar

Introduction

The four pairs of paranasal sinuses are air-filled extensions of the nasal cavity within the facial skeleton. They are lined by respiratory mucosa (pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with goblet cells) and all drain, directly or via the infundibulum, into the lateral wall of the nasal cavity. Functions include lightening the skull, warming/humidifying inspired air, voice resonance, and immune defence via mucus production.

Nasal Drainage Overview

Superior Meatus

Posterior ethmoidal air cells

Middle Meatus

Frontal sinus (via frontonasal duct)
Anterior & middle ethmoidal cells
Maxillary sinus (hiatus semilunaris)

Inferior Meatus

Nasolacrimal duct (not a sinus but opens here)

⬆ Sphenoidal sinus drains separately into the sphenoethmoidal recess (above the superior turbinate).

Sinus Bone Drains Into Nerve Supply Clinical Note
Maxillary Body of maxilla Middle meatus (hiatus semilunaris) V₂ Superior alveolar nn., infraorbital n. Largest; ostium at roof → poor drainage; tooth root pain
Frontal Squamous frontal bone Middle meatus via frontonasal duct V₁ Supraorbital, supratrochlear Most tortuous drainage; forehead pain on sinusitis
Ethmoidal Ethmoid labyrinth Ant/mid → middle meatus; Post → superior meatus V₁ Anterior & posterior ethmoidal nn. Lamina papyracea → orbital cellulitis risk
Sphenoidal Body of sphenoid Sphenoethmoidal recess V₁V₂ Posterior ethmoidal + sphenopalatine Most posterior; related to pituitary, cavernous sinus, optic canal

Sinus 01 of 04

Maxillary Sinus ANTRUM OF HIGHMORE

🦴 Boundaries

RoofFloor of orbit (infraorbital canal runs here)
FloorAlveolar process — molar/premolar roots may project in
MedialLateral nasal wall (ostium located superiorly here)
AnteriorFacial surface of maxilla
PosteriorInfratemporal surface of maxilla
ApexZygomatic process of maxilla

💧 Drainage

Maxillary ostium → Ethmoidal infundibulum → Hiatus semilunaris → Middle meatus

The ostium opens near the roof of the medial wall — the worst possible position for gravity drainage (especially in the upright position). Accessory ostia present in ~25% of individuals.

⚡ Innervation — V₂

  • Anterior superior alveolar n. — anterior wall, anterior teeth
  • Middle superior alveolar n. — lateral wall, premolars
  • Posterior superior alveolar n. — posterior wall, molars
  • Infraorbital n. — roof of sinus

All branches of maxillary nerve (V₂) via the pterygopalatine fossa

🩺 Clinical Pearls

Gravity drainage paradox Ostium is at the roof of the medial wall — the sinus cannot drain by gravity in the upright position. Cilia must actively transport mucus upward to the ostium, making the maxillary sinus the most sinusitis-prone sinus.
Dental–sinus relationship Roots of upper molars (especially 1st molar) & premolars may indent or penetrate the floor. Periapical infection or tooth extraction can cause sinusitis or an oroantral fistula.

Sinus 02 of 04

Frontal Sinus FRONTAL BONE

🦴 Boundaries

AnteriorOuter table of frontal bone (forehead)
PosteriorInner table — separates from anterior cranial fossa & dura
FloorOrbital plate (roof of orbit) + nasal/ethmoidal bones
MedialIntersinus septum (usually deviated)

Highly variable in size; may be absent (aplasia in ~4%). Always asymmetric.

💧 Drainage

Frontonasal duct → Frontal recess → Infundibulum → Middle meatus

The most tortuous drainage pathway. Mucosal swelling or polyps can easily obstruct the narrow frontonasal recess, explaining the propensity for frontal sinusitis with severe headache (worse on bending forward/morning).

⚡ Innervation — V₁

  • Supraorbital nerve (V₁) — exits supraorbital notch/foramen; supplies anterior wall & roof
  • Supratrochlear nerve (V₁) — medial aspect
  • Anterior ethmoidal nerve (V₁) — floor of sinus

All branches of ophthalmic nerve (V₁) via the nasociliary nerve

🩺 Clinical Pearls

Posterior wall breach Posterior wall is thin — trauma or osteomyelitis can cause intracranial extension (epidural abscess, meningitis, cerebral abscess = Pott's puffy tumour when subperiosteal abscess forms anteriorly).
CT landmark On coronal CT, identify the frontal recess as an hourglass-shaped drainage pathway. A patent recess = functional drainage.

Sinus 03 of 04

Ethmoidal Air Cells ETHMOID LABYRINTH · 3–18 CELLS

🦴 Boundaries

LateralLamina papyracea (orbital plate) — paper-thin; separates from orbit
MedialSuperior & middle turbinates (lateral nasal wall)
SuperiorFovea ethmoidalis (roof) — separates from anterior cranial fossa / cribriform plate
InferiorMerges with middle meatus structures
AnteriorFrontal process of maxilla + lacrimal bone
PosteriorAnterior face of sphenoid

💧 Drainage

Anterior group:

Anterior cells → Infundibulum → Middle meatus

Posterior group:

Posterior cells → Superior meatus

Middle group drains into the middle meatus alongside anterior cells.

⚡ Innervation — V₁

  • Anterior ethmoidal nerve (nasociliary → V₁) — anterior & middle cells, also supplies nasal dorsum
  • Posterior ethmoidal nerve (nasociliary → V₁) — posterior cells
  • Infratrochlear nerve (V₁) — medial contributions

Autonomic Supply

Parasympathetic

Pterygopalatine ganglion (CN VII → greater petrosal n. → nerve of pterygoid canal)

Sympathetic

Deep petrosal n. → nerve of pterygoid canal → pterygopalatine ganglion (pass-through)

🩺 Clinical Pearls

Orbital cellulitis The lamina papyracea is the thinnest bone in the skull. Ethmoidal sinusitis can erode it, leading to pre-septal or post-septal (orbital) cellulitis — risk of optic nerve compression, blindness, cavernous sinus thrombosis.
FESS target Functional endoscopic sinus surgery focuses on restoring ethmoidal drainage — clearing the ostiomeatal complex (OMC) relieves obstruction of all three anterior sinuses simultaneously.

Sinus 04 of 04

Sphenoidal Sinus MOST POSTERIOR · SELLA TURCICA

🦴 Boundaries

SuperiorSella turcica (pituitary gland) + optic chiasm above
InferiorNasopharyngeal roof & sphenoidal plane
LateralCavernous sinus (CN III, IV, V₁, V₂, VI) + internal carotid artery
AnteriorPosterior ethmoidal cells, nasal cavity
PosteriorBasilar part of occipital bone
MedialIntersinus septum (often asymmetric/deviated)

💧 Drainage

Sphenoidal ostium → Sphenoethmoidal recess → Superior meatus → Nasal cavity

The ostium is on the anterior wall of the sphenoid, opening into the sphenoethmoidal recess above the superior turbinate. This is separate from the ostiomeatal complex used by the other three sinuses.

⚡ Innervation — V₁ + V₂

  • Posterior ethmoidal nerve (nasociliary → V₁) — roof & upper walls
  • Sphenopalatine (posterior nasal) nerves (V₂ via pterygopalatine ganglion) — lateral & inferior walls
  • Orbital branches of pterygopalatine ganglion — periosteal supply

Mixed V₁/V₂ territory — the only sinus with dual-division innervation

🩺 Clinical Pearls

Dangerous sinus Intimately related to the pituitary gland (superior), optic nerve/chiasm (superior), internal carotid artery (lateral), and cavernous sinus (lateral). Sphenoidal sinusitis or tumours can cause visual loss, ICA pseudoaneurysm, pituitary compression.
Transsphenoidal surgery The sphenoidal sinus is the surgical corridor for transsphenoidal hypophysectomy (pituitary adenoma removal). On CT, look for the carotid canals bulging into the lateral walls — an important surgical landmark.

From Your Notes — Key Anatomical Connections

Pterygopalatine Fossa & The Sinuses

As covered in your notes: the pterygopalatine fossa lies "deeper, under the orbit of the eye" and contains the pterygopalatine (sphenopalatine) ganglion, V₂, and the 3rd (terminal) part of the maxillary artery.

This makes it the key relay station for sinus innervation and vasculature: the sphenopalatine artery (branch of maxillary a.) supplies the nasal cavity lining and sinuses; the pterygopalatine ganglion provides autonomic supply (parasympathetic secretomotor to sinus mucosa; sympathetic vasoconstrictive fibres pass through). V₂ branches here to become the superior alveolar and infraorbital nerves — the primary sensory supply to the maxillary sinus.

The infratemporal fossa (under the zygomatic arch) houses V₃ and the 2nd part of the maxillary artery — not directly related to sinus supply but an important adjacent space in head dissection.

Infratemporal Fossa (from your notes) — note V₃ and 2nd part of maxillary artery; the pterygopalatine fossa lies deeper, medial to this space

Venous drainage of the head (from your notes) — the cavernous sinus is immediately lateral to the sphenoidal sinus; sphenoidal sinusitis can spread to cause cavernous sinus thrombosis

Head & neck anatomy overview (from your notes)

Key Concept

Ostiomeatal Complex (OMC)

The OMC is the functional drainage pathway of the anterior sinuses — the region through which the frontal, anterior ethmoidal, and maxillary sinuses all drain into the middle meatus. It is not a single anatomical structure but a functional unit comprising:

Maxillary ostium

Opening of maxillary sinus into the infundibulum

Ethmoidal infundibulum

Funnel-shaped passage lateral to the uncinate process

Hiatus semilunaris

Crescentic gap between uncinate process and bulla ethmoidalis

Frontal recess

Superior extension draining the frontal sinus

Clinically: OMC obstruction (by mucosal oedema, polyps, or anatomical variants like concha bullosa or a deviated uncinate) causes simultaneous disease in all three anterior sinuses — explaining why maxillary sinusitis is so often accompanied by ethmoidal involvement. FESS targets OMC clearance to restore drainage.

CT Imaging Correlation

What to Find on CT

CT / head anatomy overview (from your notes) — identify sinus positions relative to orbital plates, nasal cavity, and skull base

Sinus Best CT Plane Key Landmarks to Identify Pathology Signs
Maxillary Coronal & axial Ostium (medial wall near roof), infraorbital canal (roof), tooth roots (floor) Air-fluid level, mucosal thickening, bone erosion (tumour)
Frontal Coronal & sagittal Frontal recess, intersinus septum, posterior wall thinness Opacification, frontal recess obstruction, Pott's puffy tumour
Ethmoidal Coronal (gold standard) Lamina papyracea, bulla ethmoidalis, uncinate process, OMC Honeycomb opacification, orbital fat stranding (preseptal cellulitis)
Sphenoidal Axial & sagittal Carotid canal impressions (lateral walls), optic canal (superolateral), sella (superior) Opacification, bony dehiscence near ICA, pituitary involvement
